title: Hyponatremia
objectives: Classify hyponatremia by serum osmolality and volume status
Understand the pathophysiology of ADH dysregulation
Recognize and prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome
Select appropriate treatment based on acuity and symptoms

vignette.text:
A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after her daughter found her confused and lethargic this morning. She has had progressive fatigue and poor appetite for 2 weeks. Her daughter reports she has been taking 'water pills' and was recently started on a new medication by her psychiatrist for depression.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Major depressive disorder, Osteoarthritis, Hypothyroidism

Medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily (for 3 years), Sertraline 50mg daily (started 3 weeks ago), Levothyroxine 75mcg daily, Acetaminophen PRN

Social History: Lives alone, widowed 6 months ago, non-smoker, no alcohol. Daughter visits weekly and noticed mother drinking more water than usual lately 'to stay healthy.'

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Elderly woman, lethargic but arousable, oriented to person only
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, no murmurs, normal S1/S2
physicalExam.neurologic: Lethargic, oriented to person only, moves all extremities, no focal deficits, DTRs 2+ symmetric

imaging.CXR: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality, normal heart size

questions.stem: ADH (vasopressin) plays a central role in water balance.
questions.question: Where is ADH synthesized and where is it released?
questions.hint: Consider the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.

options.text: Synthesized in hypothalamus (supraoptic/paraventricular nuclei), released from posterior pituitary
Synthesized and released from anterior pituitary

questions.explanation: ADH is synthesized in the supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei of the hypothalamus, then transported via axons to the posterior pituitary where it's stored and released. Release is triggered by increased serum osmolality or decreased blood volume.

questions.stem: ADH acts on the kidney to retain water.
questions.question: Where in the nephron does ADH exert its primary effect?
questions.hint: Consider where water reabsorption is regulated.

options.text: Collecting duct - ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels to increase water reabsorption

questions.explanation: ADH binds V2 receptors on collecting duct principal cells, triggering insertion of aquaporin-2 water channels into the apical membrane. This allows water to move from the tubular lumen into the hypertonic medullary interstitium, concentrating urine.

questions.stem: Osmotic demyelination syndrome is a feared complication of rapid sodium correction.
questions.question: What brain structure is classically affected in osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)?
questions.hint: The name 'central pontine myelinolysis' gives a clue.

options.text: Central pons (central pontine myelinolysis), though extrapontine areas can also be affected

questions.explanation: ODS classically affects the central pons (central pontine myelinolysis) but can affect extrapontine structures (basal ganglia, thalamus, cerebellum). Oligodendrocytes in these areas are vulnerable to osmotic stress from rapid fluid shifts during overcorrection.

questions.stem: Her serum osmolality is 248 mOsm/kg (low).
questions.question: How do you calculate serum osmolality, and what does hypoosmolality indicate?
questions.hint: Sodium is the main determinant of osmolality.

options.text: 2(Na) + Glucose/18 + BUN/2.8; hypoosmolality indicates true hyponatremia with excess water relative to solute

questions.explanation: Calculated osmolality = 2(Na) + Glucose/18 + BUN/2.8 = 2(118) + 95/18 + 8/2.8 = 236 + 5 + 3 = 244. Her measured is 248. Hypoosmolality confirms true hyponatremia (excess water relative to sodium), ruling out pseudohyponatremia and hypertonic hyponatremia.

questions.stem: Her urine osmolality is 420 mOsm/kg despite low serum osmolality.
questions.question: What does a urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg in hyponatremia indicate?
questions.hint: The kidney should excrete dilute urine when serum is hypoosmolar.

options.text: Impaired water excretion - ADH is present when it shouldn't be (inappropriate ADH action)

questions.explanation: Normally, hypoosmolality suppresses ADH, and kidneys excrete maximally dilute urine (<100 mOsm/kg). Her concentrated urine (420) despite low serum osmolality indicates ADH is active when it shouldn't be - either appropriate (hypovolemia) or inappropriate (SIADH, drugs).

whyChain.answer: Urine osm <100 indicates appropriate ADH suppression and maximum dilution. This suggests primary polydipsia (water intake exceeds excretory capacity) or very low solute intake ('tea and toast' diet).

questions.stem: Her urine sodium is 45 mEq/L.
questions.question: What does urine sodium help distinguish in euvolemic hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Consider renal vs extra-renal sodium handling.

options.text: UNa >30 suggests renal sodium wasting or SIADH; UNa <20 suggests extra-renal losses or low intake
Urine sodium is not useful in hyponatremia
High urine sodium always means kidney disease

questions.explanation: Her UNa of 45 is elevated (>30), consistent with SIADH, thiazide effect, or adrenal insufficiency. In SIADH, kidneys excrete sodium normally despite hyponatremia. In hypovolemic hyponatremia from non-renal losses (GI, skin), UNa is typically <20 (sodium conservation).

questions.stem: She was started on sertraline 3 weeks ago.
questions.question: How do SSRIs cause hyponatremia?
questions.hint: SSRIs are a common cause of SIADH.

options.text: SSRIs stimulate ADH release (SIADH), causing water retention and dilutional hyponatremia
SSRIs cause renal sodium wasting directly

questions.explanation: SSRIs (and SNRIs) stimulate ADH release from the posterior pituitary, causing SIADH. This is common (up to 25% of elderly on SSRIs develop hyponatremia), typically occurs within first few weeks, and is more common in elderly, women, and those on diuretics.

questions.stem: She has multiple potential causes of hyponatremia.
questions.question: Which factors are contributing to her hyponatremia?
questions.hint: She has multiple medications and behaviors that can cause hyponatremia.

options.text: SSRI-induced SIADH + thiazide diuretic + increased water intake - a 'perfect storm'

questions.explanation: She has a 'triple hit': (1) New SSRI causing SIADH; (2) Chronic thiazide (impairs diluting capacity); (3) Increased free water intake ('drinking more water'). Thiazides + SSRIs in elderly are a high-risk combination. Her normal TSH/cortisol rule out endocrine causes.

whyChain.answer: Thiazides block NaCl reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule, impairing the kidney's ability to excrete dilute urine. Unlike loop diuretics (which impair concentrating ability), thiazides preserve medullary gradient, allowing ADH to concentrate urine maximally.

questions.stem: She is confused and lethargic.
questions.question: What causes neurologic symptoms in hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Consider the effect of osmolality on brain cells.

options.text: Water shifts into brain cells (cerebral edema) due to the osmotic gradient
Direct toxic effect of sodium on neurons
Sodium is required for action potentials

questions.explanation: Hypoosmolar serum creates an osmotic gradient driving water into brain cells, causing cerebral edema. The rigid skull limits expansion, increasing intracranial pressure. Symptoms range from mild (fatigue, nausea) to severe (seizures, coma, herniation) depending on severity and acuity.

whyChain.why: Why does chronic hyponatremia cause fewer symptoms?
whyChain.answer: Over 48+ hours, brain cells adapt by extruding organic osmolytes (glutamate, taurine, myo-inositol), reducing intracellular osmolality and preventing edema. This adaptation also makes them vulnerable to ODS if sodium is corrected too quickly.

questions.stem: Classification by volume status is essential.
questions.question: How do you classify her volume status, and what does it suggest?
questions.hint: Examine her physical exam findings carefully.

options.text: Euvolemic - no edema, no orthostasis, normal skin turgor; suggests SIADH

questions.explanation: She has no signs of hypovolemia (orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, poor turgor) or hypervolemia (edema, JVD, ascites). Euvolemic hyponatremia + concentrated urine + elevated urine sodium = classic SIADH pattern. Her SSRI and thiazide are the likely culprits.

questions.stem: Her potassium is also low at 3.2 mEq/L.
questions.question: Why is hypokalemia common with thiazide use?
questions.hint: Consider what happens to sodium delivery to the collecting duct.

options.text: Increased sodium delivery to collecting duct enhances Na/K exchange, causing potassium loss
Thiazides decrease potassium absorption in gut

questions.explanation: Thiazides block sodium reabsorption in DCT, increasing sodium delivery to the collecting duct. There, the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) reabsorbs sodium in exchange for potassium secretion (driven by aldosterone). More sodium delivered = more potassium lost.

questions.stem: She is symptomatic with confusion. You need to correct her sodium.
questions.question: What is the initial treatment for symptomatic hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Symptomatic hyponatremia requires urgent but careful correction.

options.text: Hypertonic saline (3%) - 100-150 mL bolus to raise Na by 4-6 mEq/L initially

questions.explanation: Symptomatic hyponatremia requires 3% hypertonic saline. Give 100-150 mL bolus (or 2 mL/kg) to acutely raise sodium by 4-6 mEq/L. This small increase reduces cerebral edema and symptoms. Can repeat once if severe symptoms persist. Then slow down.

whyChain.answer: In SIADH, normal saline may paradoxically worsen hyponatremia. The kidney excretes the sodium but retains the water (due to ADH). NS is appropriate for hypovolemic hyponatremia but not SIADH.

questions.stem: You're planning the correction rate.
questions.question: What is the safe rate of sodium correction to avoid osmotic demyelination?
questions.hint: Slower is safer in chronic hyponatremia.

options.text: Maximum 8-10 mEq/L in first 24 hours; high-risk patients (her): limit to 6-8 mEq/L
Correct fully to 140 mEq/L in 24 hours

questions.explanation: Safe limits: ≤8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours, ≤18 mEq/L in 48 hours. High-risk patients (hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease, Na <105) should be limited to 6-8 mEq/L/24hr. She has hypokalemia, making her high-risk for ODS.

whyChain.answer: If sodium rises too fast, give D5W to relower it. Can also give desmopressin (DDAVP) 1-2 mcg IV to 'clamp' ADH and prevent further free water excretion. Goal is to stay within safe limits.

questions.stem: You need to address her medications.
questions.question: What medication changes should be made?
questions.hint: Remove the offending agents.

options.text: Stop hydrochlorothiazide and sertraline (or switch to less implicated antidepressant)

questions.explanation: Stop thiazide (major contributor) and address SSRI. Options: stop sertraline, switch to antidepressant less associated with hyponatremia (bupropion, mirtazapine), or continue with close sodium monitoring if depression is severe. Loop diuretics are safer alternatives for hypertension.

questions.stem: For ongoing management of SIADH, you consider fluid restriction.
questions.question: What is the typical fluid restriction for SIADH?
questions.hint: Intake must be less than output.

options.text: Restrict to 800-1000 mL/day (less than insensible losses + urine output)

questions.explanation: Fluid restriction is first-line for chronic SIADH. Restrict to 500-1000 mL/day depending on urine output. To be effective, fluid intake must be less than obligate water excretion. Difficult for patients to maintain long-term.

questions.stem: Fluid restriction alone may not be sufficient.
questions.question: What medication can be used for SIADH refractory to fluid restriction?
questions.hint: Vasopressin receptor antagonists block ADH action.

options.text: Tolvaptan (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist) - causes aquaresis

questions.explanation: Tolvaptan blocks V2 receptors in collecting duct, causing excretion of free water without sodium (aquaresis). Raises sodium effectively but expensive and must be initiated in hospital with close monitoring due to risk of overcorrection. Also: salt tablets + loop diuretic can help.

questions.stem: You need to systematically approach her hyponatremia.
questions.question: What is the first step in the diagnostic algorithm for hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Rule out pseudohyponatremia first.

options.text: Check serum osmolality to confirm true hypoosmolar hyponatremia

questions.explanation: Step 1: Check serum osmolality. Low (<275) = true hypotonic hyponatremia. Normal (275-295) = pseudohyponatremia (hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia). High (>295) = hypertonic (hyperglycemia, mannitol). Her osm of 248 confirms true hyponatremia.

questions.stem: You've confirmed hypoosmolar hyponatremia.
questions.question: What is the next step after confirming hypoosmolar hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Assess the kidney's response to hypoosmolality.

options.text: Check urine osmolality to assess if kidneys are appropriately diluting urine

questions.explanation: Step 2: Check urine osmolality. UOsm <100 = appropriate dilution (primary polydipsia, low solute). UOsm >100 = impaired dilution (ADH is active - from hypovolemia, SIADH, or other). Her UOsm of 420 indicates inappropriate ADH activity.

questions.stem: Her urine is concentrated despite hypoosmolar serum.
questions.question: How do you differentiate causes of impaired water excretion?
questions.hint: Volume status and urine sodium are key.

options.text: Assess volume status: hypovolemic (appropriate ADH), euvolemic (SIADH), hypervolemic (CHF/cirrhosis)

questions.explanation: Step 3: Assess volume status clinically. Hypovolemic (low BP, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes) = appropriate ADH response. Euvolemic = SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency. Hypervolemic (edema, ascites) = CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome. She is euvolemic.

questions.stem: SIADH is your leading diagnosis.
questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for SIADH?
questions.hint: It's a diagnosis of exclusion with specific criteria.

options.text: Hypoosmolar hyponatremia + UOsm >100 + UNa >30 + euvolemic + normal thyroid/adrenal function

questions.explanation: SIADH criteria: (1) Serum osm <275; (2) Urine osm >100; (3) Urine Na >30; (4) Euvolemia; (5) Normal thyroid, adrenal, renal function; (6) No diuretic use (though diuretics can unmask/worsen). She meets all criteria. Low uric acid supports SIADH (increased clearance).

questions.stem: You're monitoring her correction.
questions.question: How should sodium be monitored during correction?
questions.hint: Frequent monitoring prevents overcorrection.

options.text: Check sodium every 2-4 hours initially, then every 4-6 hours as stabilizing

questions.explanation: Monitor sodium every 2-4 hours during active correction, especially in first 24 hours. Adjust therapy based on rate of rise. If correcting too fast, give D5W or desmopressin to slow rise. Document 24-hour and 48-hour total correction.

questions.stem: 48 hours later, after sodium rose from 118 to 132 mEq/L, she develops dysarthria and dysphagia.
questions.question: What complication has occurred?
questions.hint: She was corrected too rapidly.
questions.explanation: She was overcorrected: 14 mEq/L rise in 48 hours exceeds safe limits (should be ≤18 in 48h, but given her risk factors, should have been limited to ~12). ODS presents 2-6 days after correction with dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, 'locked-in syndrome.' MRI shows pontine lesions.

whyChain.answer: Once symptomatic ODS develops, it's often irreversible, though some patients improve over weeks-months. Prevention is key. Some case reports suggest re-lowering sodium may help if caught very early.

questions.stem: Severe acute hyponatremia can cause death.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of death in severe acute hyponatremia?
questions.hint: Consider what happens in the rigid skull.
questions.explanation: Severe acute hyponatremia causes rapid brain swelling. The rigid skull limits expansion, causing increased ICP and potentially transtentorial or tonsillar herniation. Young women and children are at highest risk (possibly hormonal effects on Na/K-ATPase). This is why acute symptomatic hyponatremia is a medical emergency.

questions.stem: She also has hypokalemia at 3.2 mEq/L.
questions.question: Why is correcting hypokalemia important in hyponatremia management?
questions.hint: Potassium affects sodium levels.

options.text: Potassium replacement raises serum sodium (K is exchangeable with Na) and increases ODS risk if not accounted for
Hypokalemia is unrelated to sodium correction
Only correct potassium after sodium normalizes

questions.explanation: Potassium is an 'exchangeable' cation - giving KCl raises serum sodium (K enters cells, Na exits). Must factor K replacement into sodium correction rate. Her hypokalemia also increases ODS risk. Correct K, but account for its effect on Na rise.

questions.stem: Integrating all clinical information.
questions.question: Which statement BEST synthesizes this patient's presentation?
questions.hint: Connect the multiple contributing factors.

options.text: Multifactorial hyponatremia from SSRI-induced SIADH + thiazide + increased water intake in high-risk elderly patient

questions.explanation: This synthesis captures: multiple contributors (SSRI started 3 weeks ago causing SIADH, chronic thiazide impairing dilution, behavioral polydipsia), vulnerable host (elderly, female, recent widowhood/depression), and appropriate diagnostic findings (euvolemic, concentrated urine, high urine Na, normal cortisol/TSH).

questions.stem: Planning comprehensive management.
questions.question: What is the comprehensive management plan?
questions.hint: Address acute treatment and prevention of recurrence.

options.text: 3% saline for symptoms → stop thiazide/SSRI → fluid restrict → monitor sodium q2-4h → prevent overcorrection

questions.explanation: Comprehensive plan: (1) 3% saline 100-150mL for symptomatic hyponatremia; (2) Stop thiazide, address SSRI (stop or switch); (3) Fluid restriction 800-1000mL/day; (4) Monitor Na q2-4h, limit correction to 6-8 mEq/L/24h given her risk; (5) Correct hypokalemia; (6) Have D5W and desmopressin available for overcorrection.
